narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21. However, the admissions model we use to predict first-time bar exam success has assessed your overall risk of failure at 42.9%, which is high enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application—it's designed to flag applicants where the odds of passing the bar on the first try dip below a safe threshold for the program. The most influential factor pulling the prediction toward bar failure was your position in the third-year decile ranking of 3, which is below the average of about 5.5 and signaled to the model a potential dip in later academic momentum compared to peers who typically rank higher. Your LSAT score of 30, lower than the dataset average of around 37, also nudged the prediction toward failure risk, as stronger scores are key indicators for bar success in similar students. Your middle family income bracket played a modest role in the same direction, reflecting patterns where financial resources can impact preparation. On a brighter note, your strong undergrad GPA was the top factor pushing strongly against failure, essentially acting as a major vote of confidence in your foundational abilities, far exceeding what's typical. Being a full-time student helped a bit too, aligning with the vast majority of successful applicants who follow this path. Your first-year decile of 4, while below average, had only a minor influence toward risk. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your GPA shines as a standout strength, but the combination of your LSAT, later decile, and income created enough concern to outweigh it, especially in a model trained on diverse backgrounds like yours. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar profiles succeed through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year performance to improve decile rankings, and exploring resources tailored for Black women in law, like prep courses or mentorship programs that have helped others overcome these hurdles. You're capable of turning this around, and we encourage you to reapply once you've addressed these areas. Keep pushing—you've already proven your academic grit.
